coredata.dc:description: This work introduces an extremely easy method for preparing luminescent carbon dots (CDs) at ambient temperature using 1,2-naphthoquinone sulphonate and ethylenediamine as precursors via self-exothermic reaction without energy input. The as-obtained CDs have a high quantum yield (34.1 %), a production yield of 21.2 %, and a small size diameter (3.44 nm). Various techniques (NMR, TEM, EDX-mapping, XPS, XRD, FT-IR, fluorescence, and UV–visible spectroscopy) were used to characterize the prepared CDs. The CDs exhibited an excitation-independent emission with λ ex of 275 nm, demonstrating their homogeneity and high purity. The anticancer drug vincristine (VCR) quantitively quenched the fluorescent signal of the synthesized CDs, allowing their application as the first fluorescent nano-sensor to determine VCR. The quenching effect was linear within the range of 0.2–5.0 μg mL−1, enabling the determination of VCR in vials, plasma, and for content uniformity testing with a detection limit of 0.06 μg mL−1. Moreover, the synthesized CDs were employed as a bio-sensing platform to detect VCR in cancer cells owing to their good selectivity, excellent biocompatibility, minimal cytotoxicity, and high stability. The fabrication of CDs with excellent properties at room temperature under mild conditions paves the way for new advancements in the room temperature synthesis of CDs and offers a highly efficient alternative to traditional synthesis approaches.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work introduces an extremely easy method for preparing luminescent carbon dots (CDs) at ambient temperature using 1,2-naphthoquinone sulphonate and ethylenediamine as precursors via self-exothermic reaction without energy input. The as-obtained CDs have a high quantum yield (34.1 %), a production yield of 21.2 %, and a small size diameter (3.44 nm). Various techniques (NMR, TEM, EDX-mapping, XPS, XRD, FT-IR, fluorescence, and UV–visible spectroscopy) were used to characterize the prepared CDs. The CDs exhibited an excitation-independent emission with λ ex of 275 nm, demonstrating their homogeneity and high purity. The anticancer drug vincristine (VCR) quantitively quenched the fluorescent signal of the synthesized CDs, allowing their application as the first fluorescent nano-sensor to determine VCR. The quenching effect was linear within the range of 0.2–5.0 μg mL−1, enabling the determination of VCR in vials, plasma, and for content uniformity testing with a detection limit of 0.06 μg mL−1. Moreover, the synthesized CDs were employed as a bio-sensing platform to detect VCR in cancer cells owing to their good selectivity, excellent biocompatibility, minimal cytotoxicity, and high stability. The fabrication of CDs with excellent properties at room temperature under mild conditions paves the way for new advancements in the room temperature synthesis of CDs and offers a highly efficient alternative to traditional synthesis approaches.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are small carbon-based nanomaterials with a diameter below 10 nm. In recent years, CDs have earned massive interest owing to their appealing features, including tunable optical characters, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, stability, cost-effectiveness, and water solubility. These features are favorable for numerous potential applications, including, but not limited to, sensing, bioimaging, photocatalysis, and drug delivery [1–7]. From a synthetic perspective, CDs can be synthesized using a top-down strategy, which involves the breakdown of larger carbonaceous precursors into smaller CDs. This method can be carried out through various methods such as arc discharge, laser ablation, or chemical oxidation. Conversely, constructing CDs from smaller precursors is called the bottom-up approach. Various synthetic methods for this route were reported, such as hydrothermal, solvothermal, or microwave-assisted synthesis [1]. Despite the efficiency of the existing synthesis approaches, they usually utilize complicated instruments, consume high energy, or need very high temperatures and long preparation times.

Recent advancements highlighted the capability of synthesizing CDs at room temperature, providing a more economical and sustainable strategy. In addition, room temperature synthesis is easily scaled up, enabling large-scale production of CDs with reliable quality [8–10]. Nevertheless, the produced CDs usually have a very low quantum yield [11,12], need a long time to generate [11], and/or their synthetic process is dangerous [8] and mediated by using high concentrations of strong acids such as nitric acid [13], strong alkali such as sodium hydroxide [14–16], or strong oxidizing agent such as hydrogen peroxide [8,12,17,18]. As a result, a new room-temperature rapid preparation of CDs exhibiting high quantum yield under mild conditions holds significant importance.

Quinone derivatives have been used previously by our research group for labeling pharmaceuticals [19], aldehydes [20,21], and immunoassays [22]. In this study, for the first time, 1,2-naphthoquinone sulphonate (NQS, Folin's reagent) was used with ethylenediamine (EDA) as substrates for room temperature synthesis of CDs via a self-exothermic reaction. It also has the advantage of eliminating the need for strong acids, alkalis, or oxidants.

Vincristine (VCR), a vinca alkaloid extracted from the Madagascar periwinkle plant, is a widely used antineoplastic agent for different types of tumors, including lymphoma, multiple myeloma, colorectal cancer, and leukemia. It binds to tubulin, an intracellular protein crucial for forming the mitotic spindle. This binding effect disrupts tubulin polymerization and inhibits cell replication during mitosis [23].

VCR has been determined in various matrices by a variety of chromatographic techniques [24–30]. Other analytical techniques were also utilized such as UV–visible spectroscopy [31], electrochemical techniques [32,33], and capillary electrophoresis [34]. However, chromatographic techniques are complicated, costly, multi-step, and time-consuming [35], whereas UV–visible spectroscopy and capillary electrophoresis suffer from poor sensitivity [36,37], while the high sensitivity of the electrochemical techniques is usually hampered by using multiple electrode materials [38–42]. In comparison with these techniques, fluorescence spectroscopy offers sensitive, simple, rapid, cost-effective, and green analysis [43–46]. So far, no fluorimetric method has been published to analyze VCR. Hence, we aim to develop the first fluorescent-based analysis of VCR. Since VCR quenches the high luminescent signal of the room-temperature synthesized CDs (RTCDs), the prepared RTCDs were successfully utilized as a fluorescent probe to develop the first fluorimetric analysis of VCR in its pharmaceutical form and spiked human plasma. In addition, the excellent properties of the RTCDs, such as high quantum yield, little interference, low cytotoxicity, and biocompatibility, allowed their usage for bioimaging of VCR in cancer cells.

All fluorescence data was measured via an Agilent Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer (Santa Clara, CA, USA) containing a Xenon flash lamp as the excitation source. The device was run at a high sensitivity mode, smoothing factor = 20, and spectral bandwidths = 5 nm. Absorbance spectra measurements were performed via a Thermo Scientific (model: Evolution 201, USA) double-beam spectrophotometer. Bruker Avance-III nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrometer (Rheinstetten, Germany) was used for NMR analysis using deuterated dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO‑d 6, Sigma Aldrich, Germany) as a solvent. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) imaging of the RTCDs was performed using a JEOL (model: JEM-2100) microscope (Tokyo, Japan). JEOL JCM-7000 scanning electron microscope (SEM, Tokyo, Japan) was used for Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX)-mapping analysis of the RTCDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization was performed via a K-ALPHA XPS instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) data was recorded on a Shimadzu XRD-600 diffractometer (Kyoto, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of the RTCDs was obtained by a Tensor 27 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker Corp., Germany) in the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode. Centrifuge (Sigma, 2-16P, Germany), pH meter (Jenway 3510, UK), thermostatically controlled water bath (Cambridge Ltd, UK), and vortex (IVM-300p, Taiwan) were used.

VCR pure powder, NQS, and EDA were obtained from the Tokyo Chemical Industry (Tokyo, Japan). Vincristine sulfate® vials (Pfizer, USA) labeled to contain 2 mg VCR per vial were bought from a local pharmacy. Acetonitrile (>99.9 %) was sourced from Fisher Scientific Chemical (Toronto, Canada). Britton-Robinson (B–R) buffer was freshly prepared by combining the same volume of H3BO3, H3PO4, and CH3COOH (0.04 M each). The required pH was adjusted with NaOH (0.2 M).

RTCDs were prepared at room temperature through a facile one-pot reaction as follows: 1.0 mL of EDA was mixed with 6.0 mL of deionized water containing 100 mg of NQS and kept protected from light. After 3 h, the resulting mixture was centrifuged (10 min/5000 revolutions per minute), passed through a membrane filter (its pore size was 0.22 μm), then freeze-dried. Afterward, 2.5 mg of the dried RTCDs was dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water to prepare the working RTCDs solution, then stored at 4 °C in the refrigerator in an amber bottle.

The QY of the RTCDs was determined according to Equation (1) [47]:

where IF, A, n denote the integrated fluorescence, absorbance, and refractive index, respectively. Quinine sulfate was used as a standard (QY is 54.6 % in 0.1 M sulfuric acid). A of RTCDs and quinine sulfate solutions were lower than 0.05 at λ ex to avoid the fluorescence re-absorption effect.

The production yield was determined by weighing the solid mass that resulted after lyophilization of the as-prepared RTCDs, according to Equation (2) [48]:

{mRTCDs is the solid mass of the RTCDs, while mS is the total mass of the starting materials}.

To optimize VCR sensing conditions, 100 μL of RTCDs working solution was mixed with 100 μL aliquot of VCR standard solution (its concentration was 100 μg mL−1 dissolved in deionized water), and deionized water completed the volume to 10 mL. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) was determined at λ em/ex of 412/275 nm (F0 and F refer to the fluorescence signals of RTCDs alone and with VCR, respectively). After determining the optimum sensing conditions, 100 μL of RTCDs and 1000 μL of 0.04 M of B–R buffer were mixed with different volumes of VCR standard solution to get final VCR concentrations of (0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0, and 5.0 μg mL−1). The values of F0/F were recorded at λ em/ex of 412/275 nm after completing the volume to 10 mL using deionized water. Then, the calibration plot was graphed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for VCR assay in dosage form and content uniformity testing
ce:section.ce:para: The contents of five Vincristine sulfate® vials (1.0 mg VCR/mL) were mixed. The final volume of the mixture was adjusted to 50 mL using deionized water. Appropriate volumes were placed in a group of 10-mL calibrated flasks to get a final VCR concentration of 0.2–5.0 μg mL−1 and proceed as described in section 2.6. For content uniformity testing, the same procedure was applied using a single vial as a sample. Ten VCR vials were assayed individually, and their content uniformity was assessed according to the instructions of United States Pharmacopoeia (USP) [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of VCR in spiked plasma sample
ce:section.ce:para: Aliquots of VCR working standard solution were separately mixed with 1 mL of human plasma in 15-mL centrifuge tubes and vortexed for 0.5 min. The volume was adjusted to 5 mL using acetonitrile. After centrifugation for 15 min at 6000 revolutions per minute, aliquots of 1 mL from the upper phase were analyzed as described in section 2.6.

Hepatoblastoma (HepG2) cells (1 × 104) were plated in a 96-well dish and left to attach for 24 h, then cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium supplemented with PSA (1 × 104 U mL−1 penicillin + 1 × 104 μg mL−1 streptomycin +25 μg mL−1 amphotericin B), and 10 % FCS (fetal calf serum). After overnight incubation (at 37 °C in 5 % CO2), the cells were treated with different concentrations of the RTCDs (2.5 × 10−2, 2.5 × 10−3, 2.5 × 10−4, and 2.5 × 10−5 mg%). Following 2-days of incubation (at 37 °C in 5 % CO2), 20 μL of 0.1 % MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) was placed in all wells. After 4 h of incubation, 0.1 mL from 0.1 N hydrochloric acid acidified 10 % sodium dodecyl sulfate was added. Afterward, the optical density values at 570 nm were recorded to determine the cell viability.

HepG2 cells were plated on a coverslip in a six-well plate (2 × 105 cells per mL, 2 mL per well). After incubation for 24 h, cells were incubated with either 0.025 mg% of RTCDs only or RTCDs with VCR (3.0 and 6.0 μg mL−1) for 1 h. Then, the cells were carefully washed using phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and subjected to fixation using ice-cold methanol for 0.5 h at room temperature. The microscopic examination was performed at wavelength 450 nm, and the images were captured by fluorescence microscope (Labomed Lx 400, USA).

RTCDs might be obtained without any external energy source at room temperature by simple mixing of NQS and EDA for 3 h as shown in Scheme 1 . Upon the addition of EDA to NQS, the orange-red color of NQS instantly turned to black ( Video S1 ), and then the characteristic dark brown color of CDs gradually formed. The production yield of the RTCDs was 21.2 %, while their relative QY was 34.1 %, indicating their suitability for sensing and bioimaging applications. The QY of the RTCDs is considered one of the highest QY obtained at ambient temperature yet. Table 1 emphasizes the benefits of the proposed synthetic approach compared to previous CDs synthesized at room temperature. The proposed synthetic approach is relatively fast, avoids harsh conditions, and obtains very high QY.

RTCDs were formed owing to two reactions that occur between NQS and EDA; one is a condensation reaction between the carbonyl of NQS with the 1ry amine of EDA, resulting in Schiff base formation (CN bond), and the other one is a nucleophilic substitution reaction between the sulfonate moiety of NQS and the 1ry amine of EDA as shown in Scheme S1. NMR characterization was performed to confirm this assumption and investigate the structure of the RTCDs. The 13C NMR spectrum of NQS is shown in Fig. S1. The peaks at 178 and 182 ppm are assigned to the 2 carbonyl moieties of NQS. The peak at 156 ppm is attributed to the aromatic carbon attached to the sulphonate group of NQS [52]. These peaks disappeared in the 13C NMR spectrum of RTCDs and new multiple peaks appeared in the range of 141–147 ppm as shown in Fig. S2, indicating Schiff base formation [53]. In addition, the new peaks that appeared in the range of 44–66 ppm of the 13C NMR spectrum of RTCDs are attributed to the C–N bond [54], confirming the nucleophilic substitution reaction between EDA and NQS. Moreover, the 13C NMR spectrum of RTCDs showed signals around 30–80 ppm indicating the presence of sp3 carbon atoms, and signals >100 ppm belonged to the sp2 carbon atoms [55]. On the other hand, 1H NMR analysis further confirmed Schiff base formation. The 1H NMR spectrum of RTCDs (Fig. S3) revealed the peak at 9 ppm for imine proton, which is absent in the 1H NMR spectrum of the substrate (NQS) as shown in (Fig. S4) [56]. Furthermore, the 1H NMR spectrum of RTCDs showed peaks in the region 1.5–3.6 ppm for sp3 C–H protons, and protons attached with amine, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups, while the signal at 8.3 ppm is due to aromatic protons [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization and optical properties of RTCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Different techniques were applied to characterize the RTCDs. TEM image confirms the formation of RTCDs as presented in Fig. 1 a; the as-prepared RTCDs were uniformly distributed spherical nanoparticles without apparent aggregation and had a small particle size distribution falling between 2 and 5.5 nm (average size = 3.44 nm) as shown in Fig. 1b. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) characterization of the RTCDs was performed at different reaction times to observe the morphology of the formed RTCDs at different stages. After reacting NQS with EDA for 0.5 h, no nanodot particles were observed as shown in Fig. S5a. After 1.5 h, nano-sized aggregated particles were found (Fig. S5b), indicating a longer reaction time is needed for the complete formation of CDs [55]. The HRTEM image captured after 3 h (Fig. S5c) showed uniformly dispersed nanoparticles without aggregation, and the formed CDs revealed an interplanar lattice space of 2.4 Å, which corresponds to the (112) plane of graphite [58].

XRD pattern of the RTCDs was recorded to examine their crystallinity. As depicted in Fig. 1c, the broad peak at 2θ = 22° represents the carbon (002) plane and indicates the amorphous nature of the RTCDs [4,5]. FT-IR characterization was conducted to explore the RTCDs’ surface groups as shown in Fig. 1d. The wide band centered at 3300 cm−1 and the intense peak at 1640 cm−1 correspond to O–H/N–H and CN/CO stretching vibrations, respectively. The peaks at 2870, 1520, 1340, 850, and 690 cm−1 are related to C–H, CC, C–N, CH2, and C–S groups, respectively [59]. EDX spectrum (Fig. 1e) and elemental mapping analysis (Fig. 1f) of the RTCDs endorse that they consist of C, O, N, and S elements.

XPS spectra were recorded to further investigate the surface groups of the RTCDs. XPS full survey spectrum of the RTCDs shown in Fig. 2 a displays the typical peaks of C1s (286.30 eV), N1s (400.83 eV), O1s (533.08 eV), and S2p (169.40 eV). The C1s spectrum shows three signals at 284.14 eV (CC), 284.68 eV (C–C), and 287.84 eV (CN/CO) as presented in Fig. 2b [60]. The N1s spectrum shown in Fig. 2c further proves the presence of CN (398.59 eV), and quaternary nitrogen (400.62 eV) [61]. The O1s spectrum reveals three peaks at 530.67 eV (C–O/C–O–C), 531.31 eV (CO), and 532.40 eV (O–H) as presented in Fig. 2d [62]. The S2p spectrum presented in Fig. 2e shows the peak at 164.8 eV represents the C–S bond [63], and doublet peaks at 167.4 and 168.7 eV attributed to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 of C-SOx, respectively [64].

UV–visible absorbance spectra of NQS, EDA, and RTCDs in aqueous solutions were investigated. Fig. 3 a shows that the RTCDs exhibit a peak at 274 nm related to π-π∗ transitions, and another peak at 359 nm due to n-π∗ transitions [65]. EDA and NQS do not show these peaks, indicating the successful formation of CDs. The RTCDs show excitation-independent fluorescence emission; upon varying λ ex over a range of 270–380 nm, the fluorescence intensity altered, but λ em did not shift as shown in Fig. 3b, mostly because the RTCDs are generated only from Schiff base formation and nucleophilic substitution reaction between NQS and EDA [8].

Generally, there are two widely accepted mechanisms clarifying the luminescence of CDs known as the quantum size effect which is related to the core of CDs, and the surface state which is influenced by the function group on CDs' surface. Usually, when the quantum size effect is the predominant luminescence mechanism, the CDs exhibit excitation-dependent emission resulting from different particle sizes. In our case, the RTCDs exhibited an excitation-independent fluorescence behavior (Fig. 3b) and showed a uniform particle distribution as shown in the TEM characterization (Fig. 1a, and S5c) which excludes the quantum size effect from being the predominant luminescent mechanism. On the other hand, FT-IR (Fig. 1d) and XPS (Fig. 2) spectra of the RTCDs revealed various surface groups, particularly the CN group as it was reported that the presence of the CN group on the CDs’ surface provides new energy levels, and causes structural configurations, resulting in increased electronic transitions, suggesting that surface state is the predominant luminescent mechanism of the RTCDs [60,66].

Since the stability of CDs is a significant factor in their usage for analytical purposes, different tests were performed to assess the fluorescence stability of the RTCDs. The impact of various anions and cations on the fluorescence of the RTCDs was examined at different pH levels (3,5,7, and 9). As shown in Fig. 3c, negligible effects were observed in the presence of high concentrations (50 μM) of the examined anions and cations, indicating insensitivity of the RTCDs toward metal ions as predictable biological quenchers and proving their suitability for biological applications. Also, Fig. 3d presents the influence of pH on the emitted fluorescence by RTCDs. The signal decreased slightly at highly acidic pH while remaining nearly constant over the pH range of 4–12; such pH-independent fluorescence emission indicates good stability of the RTCDs and their appropriateness for biological applications. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of the prepared RTCDs showed good stability when the NaCl concentration was raised from 0 to 1.0 M, as shown in Fig. 3e, demonstrating the excellent fluorescence stability of the prepared RTCDs. Finally, the impact of storage on the fluorescence of RTCDs was tested. It was found to be stable for at least two months if kept refrigerated at 4 °C in an amber bottle protected from the light (Fig. 3f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: RTCDs as a nanoprobe for VCR
ce:section.ce:para: The strong fluorescence signal of the RTCDs at 412 nm was quantitatively quenched after adding increasing concentrations of VCR as shown in Fig. 4 a. Hence, the RTCDs were considered as a turn-off fluorescent probe to determine VCR (Scheme 1).

The fluorescence quenching may result from one or more different processes, such as the inner filter effect (IFE), Förster-resonance energy transfer, static quenching, or dynamic quenching. Therefore, different systematic experiments were followed to elucidate the quenching mechanism.

First, the great spectral overlap between the excitation of the RTCDs and the absorption of VCR presented in Fig. 4b suggests that IFE likely leads to the observed quenching effect. This means that the excitation light of the fluorophore (RTCDs) will be absorbed by the quencher (VCR), leading to a decrease in the emitted signal (fluorescence quenching). To determine the contribution of IFE to the overall quenching effect, each value of fluorescence intensity of the RTCDs-VCR system was corrected to remove IFE using Equation (3) [65]:
(3)
F corrected
=
F observed
× antilog
[
Absorbance
λ ex
+
Absorbance
λ em
2
]

Then, the fluorescence suppression efficiency (%E) was determined for Fcorrected and Fobserved by applying Equation (4) [65]:
(4)
% E =
[
1 −
F
F 0
]
× 100

A plot of %E against VCR concentration (μM) was constructed (Fig. 4c). A great loss of suppression efficiency occurs after the removal of IFE, indicating that the IFE process is the main contributor to the observed fluorescence quenching.

Next, the excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence quenching of the RTCDs is another evidence of the IFE [67]. Since IFE originated from the spectral overlap between the excitation of the fluorophore (RTCDs) and the absorption of the quencher (VCR), changing λ ex is expected to vary the quenching effect. Therefore, the quenching effect of the RTCDs-VCR system was investigated at different excitation wavelengths over a range of 240–340 nm. The obtained spectrum of the excitation wavelength fluorescence quenching of the RTCDs-VCR system looks like the absorption spectrum of VCR as shown in Fig. 4d, which is strong evidence of the IFE.

Then, a Stern-Volmer study was conducted to determine if another mechanism, such as static or dynamic quenching participated in fluorescence quenching besides IFE. In the static mechanism, the analyte forms a non-fluorescent complex with the fluorophore, while dynamic quenching results from diffusion and collisions of the quencher to the excited fluorophore. The Stern-Volmer study was conducted using Equation (5) [65]:
(5)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
[ VCR ]
where KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and [VCR] is the molar concentration of VCR.

Stern-Volmer study at different temperatures is useful for differentiation between static and dynamic quenching. In dynamic quenching, elevating the temperature enhances the quenching, whereas in static mechanism, increasing the temperature diminishes the quenching [65]. In this study, Stern-Volmer plots were constructed at 308, 318, and 328 K. As shown in Fig. 4e, the quenching was enhanced upon raising the temperature, and the Ksv increased regularly upon raising the temperature as shown in Table S1, which is a characteristic behavior of the dynamic mechanism.

Finally, to exclude the static mechanism, the UV spectrum of the RTCDs when water was used as a reference perfectly coincides with the UV spectrum of the RTCDs-VCR system when the same concentration of VCR was used as a reference (Fig. 3a). The lack of apparent changes in the UV spectra of the RTCDs alone and with VCR denies the formation of a ground-state complex and further excludes the static quenching [65].

Overall, it was concluded from these experiments that IFE is the predominant contributor to the quenching of the RTCDs-VCR system, and the remaining part of the quenching resulted from the dynamic interactions.

The experimental variables that alter the quenching of the RTCDs-VCR system were examined to optimize the VCR sensing conditions. First, the impact of pH was studied in a range of 2–12 using B–R buffer. As presented in Fig. 5 a, the quenching effect was slightly higher in alkaline pH compared to acidic pH, and the highest response was attained at pH 9. As a result, it was chosen as the optimal pH. Next, the influence of buffer volume was tested in a range of 0.2–2 mL as shown in Fig. 5a. The optimum quenching was obtained using 1 mL, which was selected as the ideal volume. Finally, the impact of incubation time was investigated. The quenching effect appeared immediately and remained unaltered for at least 30 min as shown in Fig. 5b. Therefore, no incubation period was required before analysis.

International Council for Harmonisation ICHQ2(R1) guidelines [68] were followed to study the validation of the proposed VCR sensing method. The calibration curve was obtained by plotting the fluorescence quenching against VCR concentration. An excellent linear relationship (r2 = 0.9982) was observed over a VCR concentration range of 0.2–5.0 μg mL−1 (Fig. 5c). The quantitation limit (LOQ) and the detection limit (LOD) were estimated as LOQ = 10 (standard deviation (S.D) of blank/slope of calibration plot), and LOD = 3.3 (S.D of blank/slope of calibration plot) [68]. The small values of LOQ (0.18 μg mL-1) and LOD (0.06 μg mL-1) reflect the high sensitivity of the developed VCR sensing strategy. Table S2 summarizes the validation parameters of the proposed method.

Six VCR concentrations within the linear range were analyzed to examine the accuracy of the developed VCR sensing strategy. The high percentage of recoveries, small S.D values, and absence of significant differences between the proposed method and the reference method confirm the accuracy of the proposed method as shown in Table S3. The precision of the developed strategy was investigated at three VCR concentration levels included in the linear range. Triplicate analyses of each concentration during the same day and 3 successive days were performed to assess intra-day and inter-day precision, respectively. The relative standard deviation (%RSD) was below 3 % in the case of intra-day precision, and below 3.4 % in the case of inter-day precision, revealing that the proposed VCR sensing strategy is precise.

The selectivity study was conducted to determine the method's capacity to quantify VCR selectively in the presence of probable interferents. As shown in Fig. 5d, the common organic species in biological matrices showed an insignificant influence on the RTCDs' signal, indicating the suitability of the synthesized probe to quantify VCR in biological matrices. In addition, the selectivity was further confirmed by calculating the tolerance limit of different co-administered drugs. The tolerance limit was estimated as the highest concentration that changes the fluorescence signal of the RTCDs by a maximum of 5 % [69], which was 12, 86, and 184 μg mL−1 for vincamine, cyclophosphamide, and metformin, respectively.

Finally, robustness testing was conducted by applying factorial design experiments with eight runs to evaluate the effects of minor experimental alterations around the optimized variables on the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F). The chosen critical parameters were pH (8.9–9.1), volume (0.99–1.01 mL) of B–R buffer, and volume of RTCDs (99–101 μL), while the monitored response was (F0/F). The Pareto chart shown in Fig. S6 indicates insignificant impacts of the investigated minor changes individually or collectively on the fluorescence quenching, confirming the method's robustness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing applications of the synthesized RTCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the proposed VCR sensing strategy was tested for analysis of VCR in commercial injectable preparations. The proposed method efficiently determined VCR in its pharmaceutical form with excellent performance, proving its suitability for routine quality control analysis of VCR. Furthermore, the results of the developed VCR sensing strategy agreed with the comparison method, and no considerable differences were found as shown in Table 2 .

In addition, it is necessary to check the uniformity of dosage units when the dose is less than 25 mg or when the drug-substance ratio is below 25 % [49]. Since the unit dose of VCR is 2 mg, it was necessary to check the uniformity of its dosage units. The developed VCR sensing strategy was applied to test the content uniformity of VCR vials. As per USP guidelines, the acceptance value (AV) was determined according to Equation (6):
(6)
AV =
|
M −
X ̄
|
+ KS
Where M is the reference value,
X ‾
is the average recovery of individual content, K is the acceptability constant (K = 2.4 if 10 units were used), and S is the samples' S.D. The proposed VCR sensing strategy was applied to 10 individual vials, and their average recovery was 99.8 ± 2 %. The AV was found to be 4.8, which is less than the maximum allowed acceptance value (L1 = 15), which indicates acceptable uniformity of VCR commercial vials.

On the other hand, despite the efficiency of VCR, its dose-dependent neurotoxicity is the primary problem that limits its use. In addition, it demonstrates wide inter-individual variability in its pharmacokinetics [23]. Therefore, determining VCR in plasma helps optimize treatment outcomes and minimize toxicity. The applicability of the developed approach for VCR sensing in spiked plasma samples was investigated since the maximum plasma concentration (Cmax) of VCR equals 1.07 μg mL−1, which is covered by the method's linearity range [70]. The results summarized in Table 2 prove the good performance of the RTCDs as a VCR sensor in plasma samples as indicated by high % recoveries (98–110 %) and small RSD%, in addition to the absence of significant differences when statistically compared with the comparison method using t-test and F-test. Values of % recovery in plasma were determined according to Equation (7):
(7)
F 0
F
= 0.1871 C + 1.0204
(
r 2
= 0.9958
)

Standard MTT assay was conducted to assess the cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of RTCDs. As presented in Fig. 6 a, more than 88 % cell viability was achieved in HepG2 cells incubated with RTCDs (0.025 mg%) for 48 h, which indicates low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility of the RTCDs against HepG2 as a model cell line. As a result, 0.025 mg% was selected for imaging experiments to maintain high cell viability.

After that, the RTCDs were used for biological imaging of VCR in HepG2 cells. Different incubation periods for imaging experiments were tested covering the time range of 0.5–2 h. It was found that the incubation time has an insignificant impact on the imaging experiments. As a result, 1 h was selected as a standard incubation time for the experiments. Fig. 6b shows that the fluorescence emitted by RTCDs (0.025 mg%) could be observed in RTCDs-treated HepG2 cells. After that, VCR was added to the RTCDs-treated HepG2 cells. Different concentrations of VCR were investigated. A significant quenching was observed after adding 3.0 μg mL−1 of VCR (Fig. 6c). Then, the concentration was doubled to confirm that the observed quenching resulted from VCR. As presented in Fig. 6d, a complete quenching was observed upon using 6.0 μg mL−1 of VCR. These findings prove the suitability of the RTCDs for imaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison between the developed VCR sensing strategy and the previously published approaches for the quantification of VCR
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the developed VCR sensing strategy was compared with the published methods as summarized in Table 3 . The developed strategy exhibited sensitivity higher than the reported UV spectrophotometric approach and comparable with most of the reported HPLC-UV methods. In addition, the lengthy analysis time, elevated cost, and utilization of harmful solvents highlight the superiority of the proposed methods over the reported chromatographic methods. On the other hand, although the electrochemical methods show high sensitivity, they require modification of the electrode surfaces, which increases the overall analysis cost, time, and complexity, and all these drawbacks are excluded from the proposed strategy.

Analytical GREEnness (AGREE) is a feasible metric for assessing the eco-friendliness of analytical methods. AGREE assessment relies on the 12 principles of green chemistry. The result of AGREE is a clock-shaped graph divided into 12 sections. Each section corresponds to one of the 12 principles of green chemistry. The assessment is denoted by a color scale (red-yellow-green) and a score from zero to one is assigned for each zone. In addition, the colored section in the middle of the graph indicates the overall assessment of the method with a final score ranging from zero to one [71]. Fig. 7 shows the greenness assessment of the proposed VCR sensing strategy using the AGREE tool. Only one red zone was found, which represents the offline positioning of the instrument (spectrofluorometer) since the production section in pharmaceuticals should be separated from the quality control laboratories. Three yellow zones were found (zone numbers 1,5 and 10). Zone 1 represents the offline sampling, zone 5 is yellow because the instrument is manually operated, while zone 10 represents the renewability of the used reagents, the methods used RTCDs as a probe and B–R buffer, the components of RTCDs are not bio-based while those of buffer are bio-based; therefore, some reagents are bio-based (yellow color). One orange zone was found, which refers to the amount of the generated waste (10 mL). All the remaining zones were green, indicating the satisfying of large requirements of green analysis by the proposed method, such as avoiding derivatization, low energy consumption, high throughput, and operator safety [72]. This was also confirmed by the overall score (0.72) and a core green color.

In this work, CDs were produced at ambient temperature, without energy input, under mild conditions, utilizing NQS and EDA as precursors for the first time. The resulting CDs have one of the highest quantum yields reported for room-temperature synthesized CDs (34.1 %). The described synthetic approach is more economical, green, safe, energy-efficient, and rapid compared to the traditional synthesis routes for CDs. In addition to very high quantum yield, the prepared CDs have excellent biocompatibility, stability, and minimal toxicity. The fascinating properties of the prepared CDs permitted their excellent usage as a fluorescent probe to determine VCR in pharmaceutical and biological matrices, in addition to bioimaging applications. The simple and efficient synthesis, coupled with the excellent characteristics of the prepared CDs, demonstrate their promising potential sensing and biological applications and open a new window for efficient synthesis of CDs prioritizing the environmental sustainability and avoiding the drawbacks of traditional synthetic approaches.
